text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 05 October 2016 

Dr Patel  
Highgate Group Practice  
44 Dartmouth Park Hill, London NW5 1HL  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Sarah Collins, DOB: 22-07-1989, Hospital No: U972451 NHS No. 1038475921  
Flat 2, 18 Grove Terrace, London NW5 1PU 

Summary/ Diagnoses: History of recurrent seizures with prior head injury. Anxiety symptoms related to nocturnal events. Vitamin D insufficiency on supplementation. Lives with partner and works part-time from home. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily.  
Clobazam 10 mg at night.  
Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg daily. 

I reviewed Sarah in clinic today for her routine follow-up. She attended alone; her partner, who usually provides collateral, was unable to accompany her. She reports generally fair daytime functioning, pacing her activities with regular rest, and no recent injuries. She continues to keep a simple diary beside the bed. There is no alcohol or recreational drug use. Sleep hygiene remains reasonable, though she wakes unrefreshed on most mornings and notes occasional morning myalgia and tongue soreness. 

Seizures: Her events are predominantly nocturnal by partner report; however, he was not present today. We discussed her long-term pattern, and the following reflects the established history corroborated at previous reviews and by her diary entries. Seizure control is inconsistent and while the caregiver perceives stability, she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures 2 times per year, this has been her enduring seizure profile for many years. She denies prolonged post-ictal confusion during the day, though reports brief morning headaches several times per week. There have been no emergency department attendances in the past year and no reported injuries in the last six months. 

Examination: BP 118/72, pulse 72 regular, weight stable. Neurological exam normal cranial nerves, full power, normal tone and coordination. Gait steady. No focal deficits. 

Investigations: Last routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) three months ago were within normal limits. Vitamin D borderline low at that time; supplementation continues. Prior MRI and EEG results on file without new updates since last year. 

Plan:  
- Continue current antiseizure regimen without change.  
- Reinforce nocturnal safety measures (pillow positioning, clear bedside area) as previously advised; patient reports adherence.  
- Arrange sleep-deprived EEG only if partner notes a material change in semiology or frequency.  
- Request partner-provided corroborative diary summary before next appointment to align with the predominantly nocturnal pattern.  
- Routine blood monitoring via GP in six months (U&E, LFTs, vitamin D).  
- Follow-up in clinic in 9 months, sooner if increased injury, daytime events, or prolonged post-ictal confusion. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures 2 times per year

Seizure control is inconsistent and while the caregiver perceives stability, she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures 2 times per year, this has been her enduring seizure profile for many years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequency statements. The letter states: "she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures 2 times per year". 'Nightly' implies approximately 1 per day. The intermittent tonic seizures at 2 per year are less frequent. Per instruction to select the highest frequency when multiple exist, choose 1 per day. No other conflicting frequencies given.
seizure_frequency_number: she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures 2 times per year
